target.title: fix(codex): stop persisting per-turn OpenClaw skills snapshot in Codex native user history (#84662)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Codex app-server (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts`) wraps the OpenClaw **skills snapshot** into the per-turn user-input text via `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` and `prependCodexOpenClawPromptContext`. Because `turn/start.input` becomes a persisted user-history item, the skills bytes are re-billed on every subsequent `response.create.input`, growing the model input by ~2,194 tokens/turn on the default `main` agent (issue #84662 root cause).
- **Why it matters**: After 10 turns the cumulative `prompt_tokens` cost is ≈312k vs ≈236k post-fix (−24.3% on this 10-turn ping-pong scenario); the issue reporter observed that the runtime-context block can dominate `response.create.input` over a longer session.
- **What changed**: Route the OpenClaw **skills snapshot only** through `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` (the same field cron and heartbeat triggers already use in production), via a new `openClawSkillsPrompt` option threaded through `buildTurnStartParams` → `buildTurnCollaborationMode` → `buildTurnScopedCollaborationInstructions`. `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` now emits the workspace section only and its per-turn wrapper is renamed from `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` to `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:` so the user-input lane is correctly labelled.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: User-editable **workspace context** (incl. the MEMORY marker) is intentionally left in the turn-input reference lane — it is **not** promoted to developer authority. A unit-test invariant pins this (`thread-lifecycle.test.ts` "invariant: workspace user-editable context never appears in developer_instructions"). Workspace bootstrap (#77372), AGENTS.md, HEARTBEAT.md, cron payload guidance, the PI runner `openclaw.runtime-context` path, and the subagent / ACP / CLI surfaces are untouched. **Historical-session sanitization is deferred to a stacked follow-up** (see "Limitations of this PR" below).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations  (codex app-server `turn/start` shape)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Addresses the skills component of #84662. This PR routes future skills snapshots through `developer_instructions` so they are not appended to Codex native user history going forward. It does **not** implement the issue's *safe interim* historical sanitizer for already-bound threads; that half is deferred to a stacked follow-up (see "Limitations of this PR").
- Related: supersedes closed #84790 (TurboTheTurtle, closed 2026-05-22) by narrowing scope to the skills snapshot only — workspace/MEMORY stays in the turn-input reference lane that #84790's [P1] prompt-authority finding was raised against.
- Related: complements merged #83454 (Eva, 2026-05-18) — skills are treated as **stable OpenClaw plugin/tool guidance** and routed through `developer_instructions`, while user-editable workspace text **remains user-reference scoped in turn input** per #83454. This is not a reversal of #83454: the user-editable surface that #83454 protected is left where #83454 put it; only the OpenClaw-owned plugin-manifest surface moves, joining the workspace bootstrap files (#77372) that already live in `developer_instructions`.
- Related: builds on merged #77372 (lonexreb, workspace bootstrap → `developer_instructions`) as the lane-precedent for OpenClaw-owned stable instructions.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause**: The shared per-turn wrapper `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` conflates two fragments with different authority + lifetime needs:
  1. **Skills snapshot** (`params.skillsSnapshot?.prompt`) — OpenClaw-owned plugin manifest text, byte-stable across a session, **not** editable from chat/MEMORY. Analogous to the workspace bootstrap files (`SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `TOOLS.md` / `USER.md`) that already live in `developer_instructions` via #77372.
  2. **Workspace prompt context** (`workspaceBootstrapContext.promptContext`) — **user-editable** workspace files (e.g., `MEMORY.md`, custom project docs). Per #84662 §Security/rank caution, this must remain quoted/reference scoped.
  Bundling both into `turn/start.input[0].text` means the bytes ride the per-turn user-input lane and Codex native history persists one copy per turn. Issue #84662 carves these two halves apart explicitly; the wider scope from #84790 collapsed them back together and ran into the prompt-authority blocker raised in that review.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No unit test pinned the authority boundary (workspace user-editable text must not appear in `developer_instructions`). This PR adds one (`thread-lifecycle.test.ts` "invariant: workspace user-editable context never appears in developer_instructions").
- **Contributing context**: cron and heartbeat triggers already populate `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` in production. The generated `@openai/codex` 0.132.0 `v2/TurnStartParams.ts` does not currently declare `collaborationMode` (the formal basis for #84790's [P1] protocol concern), but the bundled runtime binary parses and honors the field on cron / heartbeat triggers today — empirical evidence below shows it also honors it on the chat trigger.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts` (6 new cases) + `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts` (1 modified case at the existing turn-input assertion).
- Scenario the test should lock in: (a) chat trigger renders skills as `developer_instructions`; (b) chat trigger without skills returns `null` `developer_instructions`; (c) cron composes cron + skills; (d) heartbeat composes heartbeat + overlay + skills; (e) cron without skills is byte-identical to current main; (f) workspace user-editable context never reaches `developer_instructions` (authority invariant).
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is structural (wrong lane); the structural invariant pins both halves (skills present in `developer_instructions`, workspace absent from it) without needing live-model evidence to rerun on every CI tick.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **Per-turn token billing on the default `main` agent** (new turns after this PR ships): skills bytes no longer re-bill in every `response.create.input`; the cumulative 10-turn `prompt_tokens` cost drops by ~24% on the ping-pong scenario in the Real-behavior proof. Workspace-context bytes continue to re-bill at the same rate as before (~+540/turn floor).
- **Per-turn user-input wrapper text** (new turns): renamed from `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:\n…` to `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:\n…`. The block now contains only the `## OpenClaw Workspace Context` section; the `## OpenClaw Skills` section is gone (moved to `developer_instructions`).
- **Existing Codex native threads bound before this PR ships**: see "Limitations of this PR" below.
- **Snapshot fixtures**: three `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*.md` files regenerated to reflect the new wrapper text and `userInputText.chars` (≈ +15 chars from the wording change; the 6,557-char skills section that previously sat inside the user input is no longer included in the snapshot of `turn/start.input`).

## Limitations of this PR (deferred half explicitly disclosed)

This PR **prevents future skills bytes from being appended** to Codex native user history. It does **not** sanitize history for threads that were bound before the fix ships.

- **Existing bound Codex native threads** that already contain `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items from prior turns will continue to replay those items on `response.create.input` until either (a) the thread cycles to a fresh native session, or (b) the deferred follow-up session-history sanitizer (issue #84662 §"Safe interim fix") lands as a stacked follow-up PR that strips the wrapper from `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages`.
- The snapshot-fixture check (`workspaceBootstrapContextFrom` field in `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*.md`) verifies the **current projection shape** for new turns; it is **not** a regression guard for the deferred sanitizer half — old mirrored-history user items are out of its scope.
- The 10-turn benchmark below was run with fresh user IDs (i.e., fresh Codex native sessions), which is why the patched arm's per-turn `prompt_tokens` curve flattens immediately. Users on long-lived bound threads will see the curve flatten only after the next thread reset, or after the follow-up sanitizer lands.

The deferred follow-up will credit `Co-authored-by: TurboTheTurtle` (PR #84790's sanitizer logic) and is independent of this PR; this PR remains complete and merge-ready on its own.

## Authority safety (why this is not the #84790 design)

| | PR #84790 (CLOSED 2026-05-22) | This PR |
|---|---|---|
| Content moved into `developer_instructions` | skills + workspace + MEMORY | **skills only** |
| Workspace / MEMORY routing | through `developer_instructions` (with a disclaimer wrapper) | **unchanged from current `main`** — stays in turn-input reference lane established by #83454 |
| Session-history sanitization | bundled in same PR | **deferred follow-up** (disclosed above) |
| Protocol-acceptance argument | schema inspection only | schema + production cron / heartbeat parity + wire-level Codex native rollout + cacheRead ratio uplift on the same scenario |
| Authority invariant test | n/a | `thread-lifecycle.test.ts` pins workspace text never appears in `developer_instructions` |

Reading order:

- Issue #84662 §"Security/rank caution": *"Stable instructions such as OpenClaw skills may be safe to move to developer/bootstrap instructions, but user-editable workspace context should remain quoted/reference scoped."* This PR follows that caveat literally.
- PR #84790 was closed by its author after the wider scope failed the prompt-authority finding raised in that review (wrapper text does not actually downgrade message authority).
- PR #83454 deliberately moved both halves *out* of `developer_instructions` so user-editable text would not gain developer authority. This PR complements that policy: workspace text stays where #83454 put it; only the OpenClaw-owned plugin-manifest skills snapshot — a stable, OpenClaw-controlled surface analogous to the bootstrap files in #77372 — moves to `developer_instructions`.
- PR #77372 already routes workspace-bootstrap files (`SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `TOOLS.md` / `USER.md`) through `developer_instructions`; skills join their natural peers under this PR.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex app-server persists the OpenClaw skills snapshot as a per-turn user-history item in native Codex threads, causing the model's `response.create.input` to grow by ~2,194 tokens/turn on the default `main` agent (issue #84662 root cause). After this fix the skills snapshot rides `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` once per thread and is not replayed for **new turns on threads opened after this PR ships**.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw checkout at the upstream/main HEAD this PR is based on, rebuilt dist; live OpenAI Codex backend (`openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini`); two local OpenAI-compatible OpenClaw gateways on separate ports sharing the same eval state directory (one unpatched baseline, one with this PR's patch applied); a live 10-turn ping-pong benchmark as the proof surface. A separate live runtime instance on an unrelated port was observed before / during / after the experiment via `/healthz` to confirm no PID change in unrelated processes.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
- Start two OpenClaw gateways on separate ports against an eval `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, one built from current upstream/main (arm A — baseline) and one built from this PR's branch (used for arms A' and B), each launched via `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway` with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`.
- Drive a 10-turn `body_contains: "pong"` ping-pong scenario against each gateway via an OpenAI-compatible benchmark CLI, using a distinct user ID per arm (forces a fresh Codex native session per arm) for three arms:
  - Arm A — unpatched baseline (#84662 reproducible state), default `main` agent.
  - Arm A' — patched, default `main` agent.
  - Arm B — patched gateway, but a separately-configured agent with `tools.profile=minimal`, `systemPromptOverride="."`, `skills=[]` (i.e., a no-skills control).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/{run-attempt,thread-lifecycle,run-attempt.context-engine}.test.ts --run`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts --run`
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check`

**Evidence after fix (10-turn live benchmark × 3 arms, all 30/30 functional `body_contains: "pong"`)**:

| Arm | growth/turn | turn 1 / turn 10 prompt_tokens | cum 10t prompt_tokens | cacheRead/input (turn 9 steady) | uncached input tokens (turn 9) | skills.count / promptChars |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| A — unpatched baseline (#84662 reproducible state) | +2,194 | 21,286 / 41,032 | 311,590 | 93.27 % | 2,614 | 20 / 6,629 |
| A' — patched | **+535** | 21,166 / 25,981 | **235,735** | **96.08 %** | **998** | 20 / 6,557 |
| B — no-skills control (patched gateway, no-skills agent) | +540 | 19,552 / 24,412 | 219,820 | 95.98 % | 960 | 0 / 0 |

All three arms used **fresh** user IDs so each arm opened a new Codex native session — the patched-arm curve reflects the new-thread behavior, not the legacy-thread replay floor. Per the "Limitations" section above, legacy bound threads are out of scope for this proof.

- Per-turn growth on the default agent drops from +2,194 to +535 tokens/turn (**−75.6 %**); cumulative 10-turn save = **−75,855 tokens (−24.3 %)** vs the unpatched baseline.
- Arm B's no-skills floor (+540/turn) is within ±5 tokens/turn of arm A' — confirming the +535/turn residual on the patched arm is the workspace-context replay floor, **not** skills. This PR intentionally leaves that floor in place per #84662 §Security/rank caution; reducing it requires either the deferred history sanitizer or a separate workspace-routing decision (out of scope).
- Codex native rollout for the patched arm shows `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` populated with a **6,740-char byte-stable** payload (md5 `fc5d0370` across all 10 turns) starting `OpenClaw skills available for this turn:\nThese skills describe OpenClaw plugin capabilities…\n\n## OpenClaw Skills\n…`. Byte-stability is the precondition for the OpenAI prefix cache; the patched arm reaches the same **96.08 %** steady-state cacheRead at turn 9 as the no-skills control (95.98 %), a **+2.81 pp** uplift over baseline at the same turn index.
- Workspace context bytes (including the MEMORY marker) appear only in the per-turn user message under the renamed wrapper `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:` and never in `developer_instructions` — verified both at the wire level (Codex native rollout) and as a unit-test structural invariant.

**Latency**: trimmed mean **−448 ms (−8.8 %)** on the patched arm vs the baseline arm (5,102 → 4,654 ms after dropping hi/lo across n=10), both arms dominated by a single turn-1 cold-start outlier (A: 26,862 ms, A': 22,453 ms). No functional regression. We do **not** claim latency improvement at n=10; a 50-turn rerun would be the right place to make a stronger latency claim.

**Observed result after fix**: per-turn `prompt_tokens` growth on the default `main` agent drops from **+2,194 → +535 tokens/turn** (−75.6 %), within ±5 tokens/turn of the no-skills control (+540). Cumulative 10-turn `prompt_tokens` saves **−75,855 (−24.3 %)** vs the unpatched baseline. 30 / 30 functional `body_contains: "pong"` success across the 3 arms. `developer_instructions` is byte-stable across all 10 turns on the patched arm (6,740 chars, md5 `fc5d0370`); workspace / MEMORY text never appears in `developer_instructions` (verified at the wire level and as a unit-test structural invariant). No functional regression observed.

**What was not tested**:

- Long-session 50 / 100-turn scaling under this change (deferred to a follow-up long-session validation; the +535/turn residual rate predicts a ~107k cumulative save at 50 turns versus the unpatched curve, but this is unmeasured here).
- Workspace-file mid-session edit cache-bust behaviour (workspace was static across the ~30 s experiment).
- Out-of-sample models (only `openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` was exercised).
- Live Discord / Telegram production-channel turn (the OpenAI-compatible gateway was the proof surface, not a channel).
- **Legacy bound Codex native threads** that contain pre-fix `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items — by design (see "Limitations of this PR"). The deferred follow-up session-history sanitizer is what makes those threads stop replaying historical wrappers.
- Session-history sanitization itself, including the `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages` post-processing path — out of scope for this PR (deferred to stacked follow-up).
- `skills.promptChars` drifted by 72 chars between the two arms (6,629 vs 6,557) due to a bundled-plugin asset rebuild difference between the two checkouts, not the source patch; `skills.entries.count` is **20 / 20** in both arms and the same skill names appear in both arms' Codex native rollouts.
- A single turn-10 OpenAI cache-eviction anomaly on the patched arm reported `cached_input_tokens=10,624` (turn-1-like) and `uncached=15,357` against the byte-stable `developer_instructions` payload (md5 stable); turns 2–9 each showed ~96.1 % cache rate and the steady-state quoted above uses turn 9. The headline per-turn growth and cumulative `prompt_tokens` metrics are unaffected (they count both cached and uncached input). A repeat run 12 hours earlier on a prior upstream/main base reported 96.07 % at the same turn-10 index, so the turn-10 dip is a one-off platform-side event, not a behavior of the patch.

## Validation

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts --run
node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs
```

## Follow-up planned (separate stacked PR)

Session-history sanitizer for already-bound Codex native threads that already carry legacy `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items. Implements `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages` post-processing per issue #84662 §"Safe interim fix". Builds on the sanitizer logic PR #84790 contributed; will credit `Co-authored-by: TurboTheTurtle`. Independent of this PR; this PR remains complete and merge-ready on its own.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve claude-cli last-call usage
target.body:
## Summary
- keep Claude CLI stream-json `result` usage as aggregate run usage
- track latest complete assistant-message or `message_start` usage separately as `lastCallUsage`
- use `lastCallUsage` for CLI session context snapshots so summed `result.cache_read_input_tokens` cannot inflate fresh token accounting
- ignore Claude `message_delta.usage` for `lastCallUsage` because it can be output-only and would drop prompt/cache fields

Fixes #85573

## Real behavior proof

Before this patch, a Claude stream-json turn with assistant usage records and a final result usage sum exposed only the final aggregate result usage to the CLI runner. That made `agentMeta.lastCallUsage` equal the summed `result.cache_read_input_tokens` value:

```text
assistant[0].cache_read_input_tokens = 18499
assistant[1].cache_read_input_tokens = 27142
result.cache_read_input_tokens = 45641
old lastCallUsage.cacheRead = 45641
```

After this patch, the parser keeps aggregate `usage` unchanged for run accounting but also exposes the latest complete assistant-call usage as `lastCallUsage`, and the CLI runner prefers that for session context snapshots:

```text
usage.cacheRead = 45641
lastCallUsage.cacheRead = 27142
```

The first regression in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` constructs that summed Claude stream-json fixture and asserts both values separately.

ClawSweeper also pointed out that Claude `message_delta.usage` can be output-only and must not replace a complete prompt/cache snapshot from `message_start`. The second regression now covers this stream-event sequence:

```text
message_start.usage.cache_read_input_tokens = 27142
message_delta.usage.output_tokens = 6
result.usage.cache_read_input_tokens = 45641
lastCallUsage.cacheRead remains 27142
```

That is the direct after-fix behavior exercised by `does not replace Claude message_start cache usage with message_delta output usage`.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` -> PASS, 17 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts` -> PASS, 54 tests
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=16384 npx tsc --noEmit` -> PASS

## Notes
- This PR has source-level proof plus the reporter-provided live issue logs for the claude-cli aggregate-result behavior. I do not have access to the reporter's live Anthropic/claude-cli setup from this cycle, so I cannot attach a fresh external-provider run.
- The code path under test is the parser/runner/session-store path ClawSweeper identified: aggregate `usage` stays aggregate, complete prompt/cache `lastCallUsage` feeds session token accounting.

target.title: fix(feishu): report connection/event activity for health monitor
target.body:
## Summary

Implement Feishu channel health-signal reporting so gateway health monitoring can observe runtime activity correctly:

- add a status sink path from Feishu channel startup into monitor runtime updates
- report lifecycle connectivity (`connected`) and mode (`websocket`/`webhook`)
- report `lastEventAt` on inbound Feishu events
- clear connectivity on shutdown/error paths

This improves health monitor observability for Feishu channels and reduces false unhealthy restarts caused by missing runtime activity signals.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`
  - pass `statusSink` into `monitorFeishuProvider` (`ctx.setStatus` patch sink)
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.ts`
  - plumb `statusSink` through monitor options to single-account monitors
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.account.ts`
  - accept `statusSink`
  - mark inbound events as activity (`lastEventAt`) and connected
  - initialize mode/connected status for account monitor lifecycle
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.transport.ts`
  - accept `statusSink`
  - websocket/webhook start/abort/error paths update connectivity/error status
  - webhook request ingress updates `lastEventAt`

## Issue

Fixes #39488

## Test note

I could not run the full local extension test suite in this environment because `pnpm` is unavailable in the host shell. Please rely on CI for full validation.

target.title: Agents/media: restore agent-scoped and iMessage attachment roots
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the message tool dropped agent-scoped media roots for runs without an `agentSessionKey`, and the image tool could not read iMessage attachments after 2026.2.26 because `attachmentRoots` never reached its local allowlist.
- Why it matters: cron/hook sessions could not send workspace files, and iMessage attachments that passed channel validation still failed image-tool local path checks.
- What changed: forward an explicit/default agent id into the message tool path, merge iMessage attachment roots into agent-scoped media roots, use those roots in the image tool, and honor wildcard local roots during local media validation.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no new media directories were exposed beyond the existing agent workspace and configured iMessage attachment roots.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #36185
- Closes #30170
- Related #36219
- Related #36236
- Related #30363

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron/hook-style agent runs can send files from the configured agent workspace again.
- The `image` tool can read iMessage attachment paths allowed by configured `attachmentRoots`, including wildcard roots such as `/Users/*/Library/Messages/Attachments`.
- `CHANGELOG.md` now includes entries for both fixes with thanks to @ravz, @macminikenny1, and @vincentkoc.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: MiniMax image tool test fixture
- Integration/channel: message tool + iMessage attachment roots
- Relevant config (redacted): agent workspace override, `channels.imessage.attachmentRoots`

### Steps

1. Configure an agent workspace outside the default OpenClaw media roots and run a cron/hook-style send without an `agentSessionKey`.
2. Configure iMessage `attachmentRoots`, send an attachment, and use the `image` tool on that local path.
3. Validate local media loading against a wildcard root such as `/tmp/*/Library/Messages/Attachments`.

### Expected

- Workspace files are accepted by the message tool.
- iMessage attachments are accepted by the image tool.
- Wildcard local roots match local attachment paths.

### Actual

- Before this change, workspace files failed with `path-not-allowed`, iMessage attachments were rejected by the image tool, and wildcard local roots never matched.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted regression tests for message-tool agent resolution, image-tool iMessage attachment access, merged media roots, and wildcard local root validation.
- Edge cases checked: explicit agent override without session key, preserved workspace-only fs policy, wildcard root matching through the shared media loader.
- What I did **not** verify: end-to-end sends against a live iMessage account or a live cron/hook deployment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert the commits touching `message-tool`, `image-tool`, `local-roots`, and `web/media`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`, `src/media/local-roots.ts`, `src/web/media.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected local media allowlist expansions outside configured workspaces or iMessage attachment roots.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: wildcard local-root handling could over-match if non-iMessage patterns are malformed.
  - Mitigation: matching reuses the existing inbound path wildcard policy and keeps explicit-root behavior unchanged.

target.title: fix(telegram): normalize durable group retry targets
target.body:
Telegram durable retry replay currently preserves legacy `group:-100...` targets, so Telegram rejects the retry as a non-numeric chat id and the real reminder can be lost after the retry limit.

Fixes #85640

## Affected surface

- `extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts`
  - `sendText`
  - `sendMedia`
  - `sendPayload`
  - `sendPoll`
- `extensions/telegram/src/targets.ts`
  - new `normalizeTelegramOutboundTarget()`

## Scope

This normalizes legacy `group:<numeric>` targets at the Telegram outbound/retry boundary before calling the Telegram send functions.

It intentionally does not:

- expand `stripTelegramInternalPrefixes()` semantics, because that helper still distinguishes inbound/internal `telegram:group:...` forms from bare legacy retry targets
- modify the generic durable delivery queue
- add a config, option, or flag
- touch the announce path covered by #81229

## Coverage note

#81229 covers the announce path through `messaging.resolveSessionTarget`. This PR covers the durable retry replay path where the outbound adapter receives a stored `to: "group:-100..."` target and passes it to Telegram send logic.

Push-before-PR searches found no open PR covering this issue:

```text
$ gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open "Fixes #85640" --json number,title,body,url,updatedAt --limit 10
[]
$ gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open "#85640" --json number,title,body,url,updatedAt --limit 10
[]
$ gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open "stripTelegramInternalPrefixes" --json number,title,body,url,updatedAt --limit 10
[]
$ gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open "outbound-adapter.ts Telegram retry" --json number,title,body,url,updatedAt --limit 10
[]
$ gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open "Telegram retry group numeric chat ID" --json number,title,body,url,updatedAt --limit 10
[]
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram durable retry/outbound replay should send legacy stored `group:-100...` targets as numeric Telegram chat IDs so retry sends do not fail with `Telegram recipient must be a numeric chat ID`.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `wolf/telegram-durable-retry-group-normalize`, head `94fe6cf029`, using a direct `node --import tsx` runtime import of `extensions/telegram/src/targets.ts` plus repository validation commands.
- Exact steps or command run after the patch: ran this direct runtime command from the OpenClaw repo root after the patch:
  ```text
  node --import tsx -e 'const { normalizeTelegramOutboundTarget } = await import("./extensions/telegram/src/targets.ts"); const cases = ["group:-1001234567890", "group:-1001234567890:topic:77", "123456789", "group:not-a-number"]; for (const value of cases) console.log(`${value} -> ${normalizeTelegramOutboundTarget(value)}`);'
  ```
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the actual source module runtime import:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx -e 'const { normalizeTelegramOutboundTarget } = await import("./extensions/telegram/src/targets.ts"); const cases = ["group:-1001234567890", "group:-1001234567890:topic:77", "123456789", "group:not-a-number"]; for (const value of cases) console.log(`${value} -> ${normalizeTelegramOutboundTarget(value)}`);'
  group:-1001234567890 -> -1001234567890
  group:-1001234567890:topic:77 -> -1001234567890:topic:77
  123456789 -> 123456789
  group:not-a-number -> group:not-a-number
  ```
- Observed result after fix: `group:-1001234567890` becomes `-1001234567890`, `group:-1001234567890:topic:77` becomes `-1001234567890:topic:77`, numeric chat IDs remain unchanged, and non-numeric `group:not-a-number` is not made valid.
- What was not tested: I did not run the full durable queue worker/retry scheduler end-to-end; the live proof below validates the Telegram outbound/retry boundary that this patch changes.

### Live Telegram delivery proof for current head `94fe6cf029`

Validates ClawSweeper-requested live Telegram proof for `mantis: telegram-visible-proof`. The Mantis automatic request path is blocked by contributor actor permissions, so this supplies contributor-side live proof instead.

Live setup:

- Head: `94fe6cf0295fd93ba6eb0ce0a4bbcb60683c1d00`
- Method: repo-external harness imports current source `createTelegramOutboundAdapter()` and `normalizeTelegramOutboundTarget()`, then sends through the live Telegram Bot API `sendMessage`.
- Credentials: read from the local secret store; token and chat ids are redacted from logs and are not committed.
- Screenshot proof: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85656#issuecomment-4524598788
  - `https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/91932218-7853-4c83-9903-44e0495f2524`
  - `https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6d63c1fd-e2d1-4fdd-ab44-78c8854dd4a4`

Redacted live output:

```text
T+0s case=legacy_group_retry input=group:<redacted-topic-chat-id> normalized=<redacted-topic-chat-id> expect=success
T+1s method=sendMessage target=<redacted-topic-chat-id> http_status=200 message_id=12
T+1s case=legacy_group_retry result=success message_id=12

T+1s case=already_numeric input=<redacted-topic-chat-id> normalized=<redacted-topic-chat-id> expect=success
T+1s method=sendMessage target=<redacted-topic-chat-id> http_status=200 message_id=13
T+1s case=already_numeric result=success message_id=13

T+1s case=legacy_group_topic_retry input=group:<redacted-topic-chat-id>:topic:10 normalized=<redacted-topic-chat-id>:topic:10 expect=success
T+2s method=sendMessage target=<redacted-topic-chat-id>:topic:10 http_status=200 message_id=14
T+2s case=legacy_group_topic_retry result=success message_id=14

T+2s case=invalid_non_numeric input=notavalidchat normalized=notavalidchat expect=failure
T+2s method=sendMessage target=notavalidchat http_status=400 telegram_error_code=400 telegram_error="Bad Request: chat not found"
T+2s case=invalid_non_numeric result=expected_failure error="Telegram sendMessage failed: 400 Bad Request: chat not found"

T+2s proof_complete=true
```

Observed live behavior:

- Legacy durable retry form `group:<redacted-topic-chat-id>` is normalized and delivered (`message_id=12`).
- Already-normalized numeric target remains deliverable (`message_id=13`).
- Topic suffix form `group:<redacted-topic-chat-id>:topic:10` is normalized to `<redacted-topic-chat-id>:topic:10` and delivered to the live topic (`message_id=14`).
- Invalid non-numeric target remains invalid and is rejected by Telegram (`Bad Request: chat not found`), so the patch does not make bad targets falsely valid.

### Current-head Telegram pin proof for `c96d27df5e`

This validates the follow-up pin normalization path added after ClawSweeper's P2 finding. The harness is repo-external, imports current head `telegramOutbound.pinDeliveredMessage()`, sends one live Telegram message, then calls `pinDeliveredMessage` with the legacy durable retry target form `group:<redacted-topic-chat-id>`. A successful `pinDeliveredMessage` proves the PR path normalized the legacy target before `pinMessageTelegram`, because the unnormalized form fails Telegram chat-id resolution before the Bot API pin call.

Redacted live output:


target.title: fix #85124: skip Anthropic API keys for usage status
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Anthropic standard API-key profiles were eligible for provider usage fallback, so status usage could send them to the OAuth usage endpoint and show a misleading invalid bearer-token error.
- Solution: Keep standard Anthropic API keys out of provider-owned usage auth while preserving setup-token usage auth and other providers' API-key fallback behavior.
- What changed: Updated the Anthropic provider usage auth hook, provider usage fallback handling, and the provider usage auth regression tests.
- What did NOT change: Inference auth, OAuth/setup-token usage auth, and API-key usage fallback for non-Anthropic providers.

Fixes #85124

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw status --usage` no longer treats a standard Anthropic API key as a usage token for the OAuth usage endpoint.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node v24.16.0, OpenClaw v2026.5.22, worktree SHA 2522f57.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  env HOME=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-85124-evidence/after-gateway-home ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-api03-status-key OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 timeout 25s pnpm gateway:dev --port 19876 --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured
  env HOME=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-85124-evidence/after-cli-home ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-api03-status-key HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:9 HTTP_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:9 pnpm openclaw status --usage
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/provider-usage.auth.normalizes-keys.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.auth.plugin.test.ts
  env -u DISPLAY -u WAYLAND_DISPLAY pnpm check:changed
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  $ pnpm gateway:dev --port 19876 --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured
  [gateway] loading configuration…
  [gateway] resolving authentication…
  [gateway] starting HTTP server...
  [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
  [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
  [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 9.7s)
  [gateway/channels] skipping channel start (OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 or OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1)

  $ pnpm openclaw status --usage
  OpenClaw status
  Usage
  Usage: no provider usage available.

  Test Files  3 passed (3)
  Tests  45 passed (45)

  [check:changed] typecheck core
  [check:changed] typecheck core tests
  [check:changed] lint core
  Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** With a standard Anthropic API key and outbound HTTPS pointed at a closed local port, `status --usage` completed without a provider usage error and without surfacing `Invalid bearer token`; setup-token usage auth remains covered by regression tests.
- **What was not tested:** no known gaps for the patched API-key usage path

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `src/infra/provider-usage.auth.normalizes-keys.test.ts`, `src/infra/provider-usage.auth.plugin.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: Standard Anthropic API keys no longer resolve as provider usage auth, while Anthropic setup tokens from API-key sources remain eligible.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers the auth boundary before any unsupported usage endpoint request can be made.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The generic provider usage fallback reused API-key credentials after Anthropic's OAuth-only usage hook returned no token, so standard API keys could be passed to the OAuth usage fetcher.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Tests covered OAuth profile ordering but not the API-key-only Anthropic status usage path.

target.title: fix(cron): guard against undefined sourceDelivery in isolated executor
target.body:
## Summary

Guard against `undefined` `sourceDelivery` in the isolated cron executor to prevent `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'sourceReplyDeliveryMode')`.

## Problem

Commit `4c613fbfe0` ("refactor(cron): centralize source delivery plan", 2026-05-18) replaced three separate params (`messageChannel`, `sourceReplyDeliveryMode`, `toolPolicy`) with a unified `sourceDelivery: SourceDeliveryPlan` across the lazy-import boundary between `run.ts` and `run-executor.ts`.

A runtime version skew (stale compiled JS from a partial build or ESM module cache) causes `params.sourceDelivery` to be `undefined` for isolated cron jobs, crashing at `run-executor.ts:147`.

## Fix

1. Made `sourceDelivery` optional in both `createCronPromptExecutor` and `executeCronRun`
2. When `sourceDelivery` is undefined, reconstruct a `SourceDeliveryPlan` that **preserves legacy caller fields**:
   - Reads `toolPolicy` from stale caller params (`disableMessageTool`, `forceMessageTool`, `requireExplicitMessageTarget`)
   - Reads `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` from stale caller params — selects `owner: "message_tool"` when mode is `"message_tool_only"`, preserving delivery semantics
   - Reads `messageChannel` from stale caller params
   - Falls back to `resolvedDelivery` for channel/to/accountId/threadId
   - Passes `requireExplicitMessageTarget` through to `createSourceDeliveryPlan` (previously omitted, defaulting to `false`)
3. Added `logWarn` diagnostic to flag the fallback

This ensures webhook callers that disabled the message tool still have it disabled, announce callers that set `message_tool_only` keep that mode, `requireExplicitMessageTarget` is honored, and no security boundary is widened.

## Tests

11 test cases in `run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts`:

**`createCronPromptExecutor` level (6 tests):**
1. `sourceDelivery: undefined` (no legacy fields) → reconstructs with safe defaults ✅
2. `sourceDelivery: undefined` → falls back to `resolvedDelivery` channel/to/accountId/threadId ✅
3. Stale announce caller → preserves `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` and tool policy ✅
4. Stale webhook caller → message tool stays disabled ✅
5. `requireExplicitMessageTarget: true` → passed through to plan ✅
6. Valid `sourceDelivery` → works normally (regression) ✅

**`executeCronRun` level (5 tests):**
7. Stale announce caller → `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` preserved through full execute path ✅
8. No legacy mode → `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` defaults to `undefined` ✅
9. `requireExplicitMessageTarget: true` → passed through executeCronRun fallback ✅
10. Missing toolPolicy → `requireExplicitMessageTarget` defaults to `false` ✅
11. Stale webhook caller → message tool disabled preserved through executeCronRun ✅

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: All `sessionTarget: "isolated"` cron jobs crash with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'sourceReplyDeliveryMode')`. Additionally, stale announce callers would silently lose `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` and `requireExplicitMessageTarget` through the `executeCronRun` → `createCronPromptExecutor` boundary.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0, source tree at commit `65c7e3812e`

### Failing proof (upstream/main, before fix)

On upstream/main (`13deea2a`), `sourceDelivery` is **required** — no guard exists, no fallback:

```
$ git show 13deea2a9d:src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts | grep -n "sourceDelivery"
112:  sourceDelivery: SourceDeliveryPlan;       # <-- NOT optional
156:  const sourceReplyDeliveryMode = params.sourceDelivery.sourceReplyDeliveryMode;  # <-- direct deref, crashes when undefined
327:  sourceDelivery: SourceDeliveryPlan;       # <-- NOT optional in executeCronRun either
```

The test file does not exist on main:
```
$ git show 13deea2a9d:src/cron/isolated-agent/run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts
fatal: path 'src/cron/isolated-agent/run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts' does not exist in '13deea2a9d'
```

### Passing proof (fix branch, after fix)

**Delivery mode preservation proof** (demonstrates the owner-selection fix):

```
$ node --import tsx scripts/cron-execute-proof.mjs

=== PR #85249 executeCronRun Delivery Mode Proof ===
Environment: darwin arm64 Node v24.10.0

Test 1: Stale announce caller (sourceReplyDeliveryMode: message_tool_only)
  owner resolved to: "message_tool"
  plan.sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"
  plan.messageTool.requireExplicitTarget: true
  plan.messageTool.enabled: true
  plan.messageTool.force: true
  ✅ PASSED

Test 2: Stale webhook caller (sourceReplyDeliveryMode: undefined, message tool disabled)
  owner resolved to: "none"
  plan.sourceReplyDeliveryMode: undefined
  plan.messageTool.enabled: false
  ✅ PASSED

✅ All delivery mode proofs passed
```

### Vitest proof (all 11 tests including executeCronRun-level coverage)

```
$ npx vitest run src/cron/isolated-agent/run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts --reporter=verbose

 ✓ cron > createCronPromptExecutor sourceDelivery guard > reconstructs a safe delivery plan when sourceDelivery is undefined 53ms
 ✓ cron > createCronPromptExecutor sourceDelivery guard > uses resolvedDelivery channel/to for legacy callers without sourceDelivery
 ✓ cron > createCronPromptExecutor sourceDelivery guard > reads legacy toolPolicy/sourceReplyDeliveryMode from stale announce caller
 ✓ cron > createCronPromptExecutor sourceDelivery guard > reads legacy toolPolicy from stale webhook caller with message tool disabled
 ✓ cron > createCronPromptExecutor sourceDelivery guard > passes requireExplicitMessageTarget from legacy toolPolicy in createCronPromptExecutor
 ✓ cron > createCronPromptExecutor sourceDelivery guard > still works with a valid sourceDelivery
 ✓ cron > executeCronRun sourceDelivery guard > preserves legacy sourceReplyDeliveryMode: message_tool_only through executeCronRun
 ✓ cron > executeCronRun sourceDelivery guard > defaults to sourceReplyDeliveryMode undefined when legacy mode is absent
 ✓ cron > executeCronRun sourceDelivery guard > passes requireExplicitMessageTarget through executeCronRun fallback
 ✓ cron > executeCronRun sourceDelivery guard > passes requireExplicitMessageTarget=false by default when legacy toolPolicy omits it
 ✓ cron > executeCronRun sourceDelivery guard > reads legacy toolPolicy with message tool disabled through executeCronRun

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Duration  837ms
```

### Regression test (existing message-tool-policy tests still pass)

```
$ npx vitest run src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
   Duration  8.12s
```

### What was not tested
- Live Telegram cron delivery (would require a running Gateway with configured Telegram bot)
- Actual version-skew scenario with stale compiled dist/ artifacts (tested via TypeScript-level params simulation)

Fixes #85051

target.title: fix: bound gateway session memory after compaction
target.body:
## Summary
- bound Pi SessionManager in-memory transcript retention in OpenClaw runtime after compaction
- route embedded runner, CLI runner, and CLI compaction SessionManager usage through the bounded OpenClaw wrapper
- keep full JSONL transcript persistence intact while pruning stale pre-compaction entries from live memory
- preserve clearer same-process port diagnostics

## Why
Current `main` uses `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent@0.74.0`, whose `SessionManager` keeps every `fileEntries` entry and `byId` entry resident in live memory for long-running gateway sessions. For Paperclip/OpenClaw gateway runs, old pre-compaction transcript branches can stay resident and push gateway memory upward over time.

This PR avoids a pnpm package patch. Instead, OpenClaw installs an idempotent runtime wrapper around Pi `SessionManager` and uses it from the gateway/CLI runtime paths that open active session managers. The wrapper keeps the active branch from the latest compaction boundary forward, preserves latest state/custom metadata and retained labels, rebuilds indexes, and leaves the append-only JSONL file untouched on disk.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Long-running OpenClaw gateway/Paperclip sessions can keep stale pre-compaction Pi `SessionManager.fileEntries` and `byId` entries resident in memory. This branch prunes stale pre-compaction live memory through OpenClaw's bounded SessionManager wrapper while preserving the retained branch, compaction summary, current state/custom metadata, labels, and full persisted JSONL transcript.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS at PR head `0dc0ab3173fb6940617d4fbce2e769b0b5b2388b`, using the branch's OpenClaw wrapper import `./src/agents/pi-session-manager-memory-bound.ts` over published `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent@0.74.0`. The proof used real persisted JSONL session storage under a temporary session directory. `PI_SESSION_MANAGER_MAX_IN_MEMORY_ENTRIES=256` was set to make the retention behavior observable in a short live run.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module --eval '...'` from the OpenClaw worktree. The command imported `SessionManager` from `./src/agents/pi-session-manager-memory-bound.ts`, created a real persisted session directory, appended 300 pre-compaction message entries, appended thinking/model/session/custom state, appended a compaction entry with a retained boundary, appended post-compaction tail messages, then inspected live manager entries, resolved context, retained labels, and the persisted JSONL transcript.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from that live command:

```json
{
  "proof": "OPENCLAW_REAL_BEHAVIOR_PROOF",
  "branchHead": "0dc0ab3173fb6940617d4fbce2e769b0b5b2388b",
  "importPath": "./src/agents/pi-session-manager-memory-bound.ts",
  "piPackage": "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent@0.74.0",
  "maxInMemoryEntries": "256",
  "entriesBeforeCompaction": 300,
  "entriesAfterCompactionInMemory": 48,
  "persistedJsonlLines": 349,
  "staleEntryInMemory": false,
  "thinkingEntryInMemory": true,
  "modelEntryInMemory": true,
  "sessionInfoEntryInMemory": true,
  "customEntryInMemory": true,
  "firstKeptEntryInMemory": true,
  "compactionEntryInMemory": true,
  "keptLabel": "kept marker",
  "sessionName": "Memory leak proof",
  "contextThinkingLevel": "high",
  "contextHasSummary": true,
  "contextHasKeptUser": true,
  "contextHasTail": true,
  "contextHasStaleRawMessage": false,
  "persistedTranscriptHasStaleRawMessage": true,
  "persistedTranscriptHasTail": true
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The stale raw pre-compaction entry was removed from live memory and no longer appeared in the built session context. The first retained entry, compaction entry, current thinking/model/session/custom metadata, retained label, compaction summary, and post-compaction tail stayed available. The persisted JSONL transcript still contained both the stale historical raw message and the post-compaction tail, so disk history was preserved while live memory was bounded.

**What was not tested:** No full gateway soak test was run on this exact PR head. The live proof directly exercises the exact OpenClaw runtime wrapper now used by the embedded runner, CLI runner, and CLI compaction paths, and automated tests cover the regression path and port diagnostics.

## Tests
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-session-manager-memory.test.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm lint`
- `pnpm deps:patches:check`
- `git diff --check && node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`

target.title: refactor: move runtime state to SQLite
target.body:
## Summary

This PR reopens the database-first runtime-state refactor after the accidental direct landing in #78595 was reverted from `main`. It moves active OpenClaw runtime state away from scattered JSON, JSONL, lock files, cache files, and sidecar stores into a typed SQLite layout, while keeping user configuration file-backed.

The main contract is intentionally simple:

- Global control-plane state lives in `~/.openclaw/state/openclaw.sqlite`.
- Per-agent data-plane state lives in `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite`.
- `openclaw.json`, plugin manifests, external provider credential files, and user workspaces do not move into SQLite.
- Legacy JSON, JSONL, lock, and sidecar files are doctor migration inputs only. Runtime startup and hot paths should not dual-read or bridge old file state.
- Session/transcript identity is relational: `{ agentId, sessionId }` plus explicit conversation/session metadata. Runtime code must not pass transcript file paths or pseudo-locators such as `sqlite-transcript://...`.

## What changed

- Adds the shared global state database opener, schema, generated Kysely types, transaction helpers, WAL maintenance, permissions handling, and state-lock helpers.
- Adds the per-agent database opener, schema, generated Kysely types, agent database registry rows, and typed session/transcript/VFS/artifact stores.
- Replaces active session, transcript, cron, task, subagent, plugin-state, pairing, diagnostic, media, backup, commitment, delivery queue, and capture stores with SQLite-backed implementations.
- Moves legacy file import/cleanup into `openclaw doctor --fix` style migration surfaces instead of runtime startup compatibility paths.
- Adds database-first guardrails: generated schema checks, Kysely linting, legacy-store scanners, and docs describing the new storage boundary.
- Updates bundled plugins and native apps to use the new state boundaries instead of private file paths where they need durable runtime state.
- Keeps dependency/package-manager/engine/lockfile policy unchanged from `main` for this cleanup PR.

## Architecture

Global database: `state/openclaw.sqlite`

- Owns shared gateway/control-plane state.
- Tracks registered agent databases and global schema metadata.
- Stores auth profile runtime state, pairing/device/node state, plugin state/blob data, scheduler state, task/flow ledgers, delivery queues, backup/migration records, sandbox registry, restart handoff state, diagnostic rows, media blobs, and shared app/plugin runtime state.
- Provides the shared `state_leases` table used by SQLite-backed lock helpers.

Agent database: `agents/<agentId>/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite`

- Owns agent-local data-plane state.
- Stores sessions, conversation identity, session routing, transcript events, transcript event identities, transcript snapshots, VFS entries, tool/run artifacts, ACP parent stream events, trajectory runtime rows, memory index rows, embedding cache, and generic agent cache entries.
- Lets large per-agent state move out of the shared gateway write lane while still preserving a global registry in `agent_databases`.

Proxy capture database: `src/proxy-capture/schema.sql`

- Keeps the focused capture-store schema for sessions/events/blobs.
- The same capture tables are also present in the global schema for managed state paths that need to live under `openclaw.sqlite`.

## Schema files

- `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql`: global control-plane schema, 62 tables.
- `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.generated.ts`: generated SQL module for the global schema.
- `src/state/openclaw-state-db.generated.d.ts`: generated Kysely types for the global schema.
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-schema.sql`: per-agent data-plane schema, 19 tables.
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-schema.generated.ts`: generated SQL module for the per-agent schema.
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.generated.d.ts`: generated Kysely types for the per-agent schema.
- `src/proxy-capture/schema.sql`: standalone proxy capture schema, 3 tables.
- `src/proxy-capture/schema.generated.ts` and `src/proxy-capture/db.generated.d.ts`: generated SQL/types for proxy capture.

Across these schema files there are 84 tables and 110 explicit indexes/unique indexes.

## Global database schema

Core metadata and locking:

- `schema_meta`: database role, schema version, app version, timestamps.
- `agent_databases`: registry of per-agent database paths, schema versions, last-seen timestamps, and size hints.
- `state_leases`: scoped durable lease/lock rows with owner, heartbeat, expiry, and payload.

Auth, diagnostics, and approvals:

- `auth_profile_stores`, `auth_profile_state`: runtime auth profile store/state JSON by store key.
- `diagnostic_events`, `diagnostic_stability_bundles`: scoped diagnostic payloads and generated stability bundles.
- `exec_approvals_config`: persisted exec approval configuration and derived counts.
- `config_health_entries`: last-known-good/promoted config health state.

Pairing, device, node, and native app state:

- `device_pairing_pending`, `device_pairing_paired`: device pairing requests and approved devices.
- `device_bootstrap_tokens`: bootstrap tokens plus issued/redeemed profile metadata.
- `node_pairing_pending`, `node_pairing_paired`: node pairing requests and approved nodes.
- `device_identities`, `device_auth_tokens`: device keypairs and role-scoped tokens.
- `android_notification_recent_packages`: Android notification recent-package ordering.
- `macos_port_guardian_records`: macOS port guardian records.
- `workspace_setup_state`: workspace setup lifecycle state.
- `native_hook_relay_bridges`: native hook relay bridge metadata and expiry.
- `node_host_config`: node-host config state.

Models, media, push, and voice wake:

- `model_capability_cache`: provider/model capability and cost cache.
- `agent_model_catalogs`: generated model catalogs per agent dir.
- `managed_outgoing_image_records`: managed outgoing image attachment metadata.
- `web_push_subscriptions`, `web_push_vapid_keys`: web push subscription and VAPID key state.
- `apns_registrations`: APNS registration rows for native nodes.
- `voicewake_triggers`, `voicewake_routing_config`, `voicewake_routing_routes`: voice wake trigger and routing state.

Plugin and channel state:

- `installed_plugin_index`: persisted installed-plugin index and diagnostics.
- `plugin_state_entries`: JSON plugin state by plugin, namespace, and key.
- `plugin_blob_entries`: binary plugin blobs by plugin, namespace, and key.
- `channel_pairing_requests`, `channel_pairing_allow_entries`: channel pairing codes and allow entries.
- `plugin_binding_approvals`: plugin/channel/account binding approvals.
- `current_conversation_bindings`: current channel conversation bindings to target agent/session rows.

Gateway, replay, delivery, and runtime queues:

- `gateway_restart_sentinel`, `gateway_restart_intent`, `gateway_restart_handoff`: restart continuity and supervisor handoff state.
- `acp_replay_sessions`, `acp_replay_events`: ACP replay session/event ledger.
- `delivery_queue_entries`: durable delivery queue with retry, failure, session, channel, target, and recovery metadata.
- `command_log_entries`: durable command log entries.
- `tui_last_sessions`: last active session per TUI scope.

Tasks, flows, subagents, cron, and commitments:

- `task_runs`: durable task run state.
- `task_delivery_state`: notification state per task.
- `flow_runs`: task-flow/controller state.
- `subagent_runs`: child-agent lifecycle, archive, announcement, frozen-result, and final-delivery state.
- `cron_jobs`: scheduler job definitions plus runtime state.
- `cron_run_logs`: scheduler run history.
- `commitments`: follow-up commitment records and delivery lifecycle.

Storage, backup, migration, sandbox, and capture:

- `media_blobs`: managed media blob storage.
- `skill_uploads`: temporary skill upload archives with idempotency/expiry.
- `sandbox_registry_entries`: sandbox/container registry state.
- `migration_runs`, `migration_sources`: doctor migration run/source ledger.
- `backup_runs`: backup archive manifest records.
- `capture_sessions`, `capture_events`, `capture_blobs`: managed proxy-capture session/event/blob storage.
- `update_check_state`: update notification/auto-install state.

## Per-agent database schema

Session and conversation identity:

- `schema_meta`: agent database role, schema version, agent id, app version, timestamps.
- `sessions`: canonical session metadata, status, channel/account/model metadata, parent/spawn identity, and primary conversation reference.
- `session_routes`: maps stable session keys to canonical session ids.
- `conversations`: typed channel/account/conversation identity with direct/group/channel kind and thread/native ids.
- `session_conversations`: many-to-many session/conversation roles with one primary conversation per session.
- `session_entries`: compatibility/display entry JSON keyed by session key, linked to canonical session id.

Transcripts and trajectory:

- `transcript_events`: append-only transcript event stream keyed by `(session_id, seq)`.
- `transcript_event_identities`: event id, parent/idempotency identity, tail indexes, and event type metadata.
- `transcript_snapshots`: explicit transcript snapshot records with reason/count/metadata.
- `trajectory_runtime_events`: runtime trajectory rows linked to session/run identity.
- `acp_parent_stream_events`: ACP parent stream event ledger by run id and sequence.

Agent-local workspace, artifacts, memory, and cache:

- `vfs_entries`: virtual filesystem namespace/path records with optional blob content and metadata.
- `tool_artifacts`: tool output artifacts by run id and artifact id.
- `run_artifacts`: run artifacts by run id and path.
- `memory_index_meta`: memory index provider/model/config metadata.
- `memory_index_sources`: indexed memory/session sources.
- `memory_index_chunks`: indexed text chunks with embedding blobs and source/session references.
- `memory_embedding_cache`: provider/model/provider-key/hash embedding cache.
- `cache_entries`: generic agent-local cache rows with JSON/blob payloads and optional expiry.

## Runtime contract changes reviewers should focus on

- Runtime code should pass `{ agentId, sessionId }` or `{ agentId, sessionKey }`, not transcript file paths.
- `sessions.session_scope`, `sessions.primary_conversation_id`, `conversations`, and `session_conversations` are the typed routing source of truth. `session_key` remains a routing/display key, not a parsing surface for hidden provider identity.
- Active transcript read/write paths should use `transcript_events` and `transcript_event_identities`; JSONL transcript helpers should be limited to doctor import/export/debug materialization.
- Legacy `sessions.json`, `.jsonl`, `.jsonl.lock`, pruning/truncation files, auth profile files, model catalog files, and old sidecar stores should appear only in doctor migration, backup/import, or explicit legacy tests.
- SQLite schema changes must be reflected in generated SQL modules and Kysely database types.
- Kysely is the expected typed query boundary. Raw SQLite use should stay in low-level openers, transaction helpers, generated-schema plumbing, or deliberately tiny runtime wrappers.

## Important non-goals

- This does not move `openclaw.json` into SQLite.
- This does not move plugin manifests or user workspaces into SQLite.
- This does not introduce runtime dual-read compatibility for old file state.
- This does not add shipped database migrations for the new layout; schema version remains `1` because this layout has not shipped yet.
- This does not change dependency/package-manager/engine policy from `main`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: database-first SQLite runtime refactor is back in a clean review PR after the accidental direct landing was reverted from `main`.
- Real environment tested: local macOS checkout on Node/pnpm, using a fresh temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME` so runtime state is isolated from the maintainer profile.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) pnpm openclaw doctor`
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the real OpenClaw CLI run after this patch:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<temp-openclaw-home> pnpm openclaw doctor
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor
[openclaw] Building TypeScript (dist is stale: missing_build_stamp - build stamp missing).
runtime-postbuild: plugin SDK root alias completed in 1ms
runtime-postbuild: bundled plugin metadata completed in 137ms
runtime-postbuild: official channel catalog completed in 5ms
runtime-postbuild: bundled plugin runtime overlay completed in 221ms
runtime-postbuild: static extension assets completed in 21ms
runtime-postbuild: stable root runtime imports completed in 136ms
runtime-postbuild: stable root runtime aliases completed in 25ms
runtime-postbuild: legacy root runtime compat aliases completed in 5ms
runtime-postbuild: legacy CLI exit compat chunks completed in 0ms
OpenClaw doctor
State integrity:
- Active state dir: $OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw
Gateway connection:
- Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
- Source: local loopback
- Config: <temp-openclaw-home>/.openclaw/openclaw.json
Doctor complete.
```

- Observed result after fix: the CLI built and completed the doctor path against the isolated temp state directory; no dependency install or package-manager mutation was needed.
- What was not tested: no broad Docker/live gateway run in this cleanup PR pass.

## Verification

- `pnpm check:database-first-legacy-stores`
- `pnpm db:kysely:check`
- `pnpm lint:kysely`
- `git diff --check && node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`
- Custom package-manifest proof: dependency/package-manager/engine fields unchanged across 13 changed package manifests.

## Review notes

- The diff is intentionally large because it is the reapply of the reverted database-first branch plus one cleanup commit.
- Commit 1 reapplies the SQLite runtime-state refactor.
- Commit 2 removes a duplicate Docker test script.
- Main revert commit: 694ca50e97 (`Revert "refactor: move runtime state to SQLite"`).
- Supersedes the merged/reverted PR #78595 with a clean reviewable PR.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(discord): keep forced voice consult diagnostics private
target.body:
## Summary
- Stop forced Discord realtime consult fallbacks from appending internal consult-policy diagnostics to the agent-facing voice prompt.
- Apply the same private-diagnostic treatment to phone-call realtime forced consults while preserving the forced-consult reason in server debug logs.
- Add Discord voice regression coverage and update voice-call expectations; add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts extensions/discord/src/voice/manager.e2e.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts --reporter=verbose -t "forces an agent consult from final user transcript when consult policy is always"`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/discord/src/voice/realtime.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Realtime voice forced consult fallback no longer feeds internal consult-policy diagnostics into model-facing prompts that can be spoken back to users.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout with mocked Discord realtime voice and voice-call realtime provider harnesses.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the Vitest commands listed above after moving the patch onto a clean branch from origin/main; after the review-comment fix, reran the Discord e2e file, the focused voice-call forced-consult regression, oxfmt check, and `git diff --check`.
Evidence after fix: Discord regression asserts the agent command message is exactly `What?` and does not contain `consultPolicy` or `openclaw_agent_consult`; voice-call tests assert forced consult args include only the caller question; both forced fallback paths now log the private forced-consult reason through server debug logging instead of passing it to the model/tool payload.
Observed result after fix: Discord voice e2e file passed; voice-call forced-consult regression passed; diff whitespace check passed; touched files are oxfmt-clean; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings on the original patch.
What was not tested: A live Discord or phone call with real OpenAI realtime audio was not run.
